section.title: Jesus Appears and the Ascension
section.sectionRationale: As the disciples talk of the reports, Jesus stands among them and says, Peace be with you. To calm their terror He shows His hands and feet, invites their touch, and eats broiled fish, proving He is risen in body, not a ghost. He opens their minds to understand that all the Scriptures foretold His suffering, rising, and the worldwide preaching of repentance and forgiveness in His name. They are His witnesses, to wait for power from on high. Then He leads them out, blesses them, and is carried up into heaven, and they return to Jerusalem with great joy.
section.thesis: The risen Jesus is truly, bodily alive, the fulfillment of all the Scriptures, who commissions His witnesses to preach repentance and forgiveness to all nations in the power of the Spirit, and who ascends in blessing so that His people worship and wait with great joy.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the bodily appearance to the disciples; the opening of their minds and the commission to be witnesses; and the ascension with blessing, worship, and joy.

units.label: The risen Lord appears in body
units.note: touch Me and see, a spirit has no flesh and bones

verses.text: While they were describing these events, Jesus Himself stood among them and said, “{{eirene|Peace}} be with you.”
But they were startled and frightened, thinking they had seen a spirit.
“Why are you troubled,” Jesus asked, “and why do doubts arise in your hearts?
Look at My hands and My feet. It is I Myself. Touch Me and see, for a spirit does not have flesh and bones, as you see I have.”
And when He had said this, He showed them His hands and feet.
While they were still in disbelief because of their joy and amazement, He asked them, “Do you have anything here to eat?”
So they gave Him a piece of broiled fish,
and He took it and ate it in front of them.

units.label: The opened mind and the commission
units.note: you are witnesses of these things

verses.text: Jesus said to them, “These are the words I spoke to you while I was still with you: Everything must be fulfilled that is written about Me in the Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms.”
Then He opened their minds to understand the Scriptures.
And He told them, “This is what is written: The Christ will suffer and rise from the dead on the third day,
and in His name {{metanoia|repentance}} and forgiveness of sins will be proclaimed to all nations, beginning in Jerusalem.
And behold, I am sending the promise of My Father upon you. But remain in the city until you have been clothed with {{dynamis|power}} from on high.”

units.label: The ascension
units.note: He blessed them and was carried up into heaven

verses.text: When Jesus had led them out as far as Bethany, He lifted up His hands and blessed them.
While He was blessing them, He left them and was carried up into heaven.
And they worshiped Him and returned to Jerusalem with great joy,
praising God continually in the temple.

eirene.gloss: Peace, the wholeness of the Hebrew shalom. The risen Christ's first word to His frightened disciples is peace, the reconciliation with God that His death and resurrection have secured. To those who had failed and fled, He comes not in reproach but in peace, the fruit of the cross now poured out on His own.

metanoia.gloss: Repentance, a change of mind and turning of the whole life to God. The risen Christ commissions the preaching of repentance and forgiveness in His name to all nations. The gospel calls the world to turn, and offers, in Jesus' name, the pardon that turning receives; this is the mission flowing from the cross and empty tomb.

martys.gloss: Witness, one who testifies to what he has seen. The disciples are witnesses of the suffering, resurrection, and their scriptural fulfillment. Their task is not to invent but to testify, to tell what they have seen and heard of the risen Christ, and the church still stands on their eyewitness testimony.

dynamis.gloss: Power, might. The witnesses are to wait until they are clothed with power from on high, the coming of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost. The mission is not carried out by human strength but by the power of God's Spirit, without whom the witnesses could accomplish nothing.

basic.context: As the disciples are still talking about the resurrection reports, Jesus suddenly stands among them and says, Peace be with you. They are terrified, thinking they see a ghost, so He shows them the wounds in His hands and feet, invites them to touch Him, and eats a piece of broiled fish to prove He is truly, bodily alive. Then He tells them that everything written about Him in the Law, the Prophets, and the Psalms had to be fulfilled, and He opens their minds to understand the Scriptures: the Christ would suffer and rise, and repentance and forgiveness would be preached in His name to all nations, beginning in Jerusalem. They are His witnesses, and He will send the promise of the Father, so they must wait in the city until they are clothed with power from on high. Finally He leads them out to Bethany, lifts His hands, blesses them, and is carried up into heaven, and they return to Jerusalem worshiping Him with great joy.
basic.hermeneutics: The passage secures two things: the reality of the bodily resurrection, proved by touch and eating, and the mission that flows from it, grounded in the whole of Scripture. The ascension is not loss but exaltation, and the disciples' joy shows that Christ's departure inaugurates the age of the Spirit and the worldwide gospel.
basic.originalLanguages: The risen Lord's first word is eirene, peace. He commissions the preaching of metanoia, repentance, and forgiveness to all nations. The disciples are His martys, witnesses. And they must wait for dynamis, power, from on high, the coming Spirit.
basic.typology: The risen body of Jesus is the firstfruits of the resurrection all His people await. The blessing with lifted hands echoes the priestly blessing of Aaron, Jesus ascending as the great High Priest. The going up into heaven fulfills the enthronement of the Son of Man at God's right hand.
basic.christ: Jesus is the risen Lord in a real, glorified body, the fulfillment of all the Scriptures, the commissioning King who sends His witnesses, and the ascending High Priest who blesses His people as He is exalted to heaven. His departure is His enthronement.
basic.god: God fulfills every word of the Scriptures in His Son, offers repentance and forgiveness to all nations, and sends the promised Spirit to empower His people. He receives His ascended Son and is worshiped with joy by those who now await the Spirit.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 1 gives the fuller account of the ascension and the promise of the Spirit. Acts 2 records the coming of power at Pentecost. Leviticus 9 shows the priest lifting hands to bless. Psalm 110 seats the ascended Lord at God's right hand.
basic.oneStory: The Gospel closes where the church's mission begins: the risen Christ, truly alive, opens the Scriptures, commissions witnesses to preach repentance and forgiveness to all nations, and ascends in blessing to reign. The story of Jesus flows out into the story of the Spirit-empowered church, and the disciples' great joy is the keynote of the age to come.

context.literary: This closing scene rounds off Luke's Gospel and sets up its sequel, Acts, which opens with the same events. Luke stresses the physical reality of the resurrection against any notion of a mere vision, and he frames the mission and the promise of the Spirit that Acts will unfold. The Gospel ends, as it began, in the temple, now filled with joyful praise.
context.historical: A first-century audience would know that a ghost or vision was one thing and a body that could be touched and could eat quite another; Luke presses the bodily proofs deliberately. Bethany lay just over the Mount of Olives from Jerusalem. Lifting the hands to bless was the priestly gesture. The command to wait in the city for the Spirit is fulfilled at Pentecost in Acts 2.
context.canonical: The bodily resurrection is foundational to Christian hope, the firstfruits of the general resurrection in 1 Corinthians 15. The commission to preach to all nations echoes the Great Commission and drives the book of Acts. The ascension and the sending of the Spirit are central to the New Testament's account of Christ's reign and the church's mission.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To prove the bodily reality of Jesus' resurrection, to ground the worldwide mission of repentance and forgiveness in the fulfillment of all the Scriptures, and to present the ascension as Christ's exaltation that sends His people out in the Spirit's power with worship and great joy.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The appearance and ascension are narrated historical events. The commission to preach repentance and forgiveness to all nations, and the command to wait for the Spirit's power, are directly prescriptive for the church, and the disciples' worship and joy model the fitting response to the ascended Christ.

debates.question: Why does Luke stress the physical proofs of the resurrection?

views.label: To affirm a real, bodily resurrection against a mere vision
views.case: By having Jesus show His wounds, invite touch, and eat fish, Luke rules out the idea that the disciples saw only a ghost or had a spiritual experience. The resurrection is bodily, the same Jesus who was crucified now alive in a glorified body, which grounds the hope of the believers' own bodily resurrection.

views.label: To assure failing disciples of a real reconciliation
views.case: The proofs come wrapped in the word 'peace' and address their fear and doubt; Jesus meets them not as a phantom but as their living Lord who has truly overcome death for them. The physical reality guarantees that the peace and forgiveness He brings are real, not imagined.

debates.bearing: Both affirm that the resurrection is bodily and real, the sure foundation of the gospel's peace, the church's mission, and the believer's hope of resurrection life.

debates.question: What is the significance of the ascension for Luke?

views.label: The exaltation and enthronement of Christ
views.case: Being carried up into heaven is the visible enthronement of the risen Lord at God's right hand, fulfilling Psalm 110 and His own words before the Sanhedrin. It is not a farewell of defeat but the coronation of the King who now reigns and sends His Spirit.

views.label: The commissioning that launches the church's mission
views.case: The ascension hands the mission to the disciples: they are witnesses who must wait for the Spirit and then take the gospel to all nations. Christ's bodily absence makes way for the Spirit's presence and the worldwide spread of the gospel in Acts.

debates.bearing: Both hold that the ascension is triumph, not loss: the King is enthroned and, from heaven, empowers and sends His people, which is why they return with great joy.

study.originalLanguages: The first word of the risen Christ to His frightened, failed disciples is eirene, peace, the shalom of reconciliation with God that His death and resurrection have won, so He comes to them not in reproach but in peace. Having proved He is truly and bodily alive, He commissions the worldwide preaching of metanoia, repentance, the turning of the whole life to God, together with the forgiveness of sins offered in His name to all nations. The disciples are appointed His martys, His witnesses, not inventors of a message but testifiers to what they have seen of the crucified and risen Lord. And they are not to go in their own strength but to wait until they are clothed with dynamis, power, from on high, the Holy Spirit poured out at Pentecost, for the mission belongs to God and is carried only by His power. Peace, repentance, witness, and power are the closing notes of the Gospel and the opening notes of the church.

typology.type: The firstfruits offered to God as pledge of the harvest
typology.fulfillment: The risen, bodily Jesus as the firstfruits of the resurrection of all His people, Luke 24:39-43.

typology.type: The priest lifting his hands to bless the people, Leviticus 9:22
typology.fulfillment: Jesus lifting His hands to bless the disciples as He ascends, the great High Priest, Luke 24:50-51.

typology.type: The Son of Man enthroned at God's right hand, Daniel 7 and Psalm 110
typology.fulfillment: Jesus carried up into heaven, exalted to reign, Luke 24:51.

study.christ: Jesus is the risen Lord in a real, glorified body, the same crucified Jesus, His wounds still visible, now alive and able to be touched and to eat, the firstfruits of the resurrection His people will share. He is the fulfillment of the whole of Scripture, the Christ who had to suffer and rise, and the one who opens the minds of His disciples to understand it. He is the commissioning King who sends His witnesses to all nations with repentance and forgiveness in His name, and He is the ascending High Priest who blesses His people with lifted hands as He is carried into heaven to reign. His departure is no abandonment but His enthronement, and He sends the promise of the Father to clothe His church with power.
study.god: The God who raised Jesus now fulfills in Him every word of the Law, the Prophets, and the Psalms, showing that the whole of Scripture was always moving toward this Christ and this mission. He is the God who offers repentance and forgiveness of sins to all nations, flinging the gospel wide beyond Israel to the ends of the earth. He is the Father who sends the promised Spirit to clothe His witnesses with power, so that the mission is His work in His strength. And He receives His ascended Son with honor and is worshiped with great joy by the disciples in the temple. This is a God of fulfilled promise, worldwide mercy, and Spirit-given power.

crossReferences.note: The fuller account of the ascension and the promise of the Spirit, continuing Luke's narrative.
The coming of the Spirit at Pentecost, the power from on high the disciples awaited.
Aaron lifted his hands toward the people and blessed them, the priestly gesture Jesus makes at His ascension.
The LORD said to my Lord, sit at My right hand, the enthronement the ascension enacts.

study.oneStory: The Gospel of Luke closes where the mission of the church begins: the risen Christ, truly and bodily alive, opens the Scriptures to show that His suffering and rising were always the plan, commissions His witnesses to carry repentance and forgiveness to all nations, and ascends in priestly blessing to reign at God's right hand. The story of Jesus now flows out into the story of the Spirit-empowered church, and the disciples' great joy and continual praise in the temple are the fitting first notes of the age of the gospel, sounding on until the King returns.

quiz.q: How does Jesus prove to the frightened disciples that He is truly risen?

options.text: He shows them His hands and feet, invites their touch, and eats broiled fish before them.
options.note: Luke 24:39-43 gives the bodily proofs of touch and eating.

options.text: He shows them the wounds in His hands and feet.
options.note: True, but it omits the further proof of eating food to show He is not a ghost.

options.text: He explains that He is a spirit returned from the dead.
options.note: The opposite: He insists a spirit has no flesh and bones, as He does.

options.text: He simply tells them to believe without any proof.
options.note: He offers concrete physical evidence of His bodily resurrection.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say the Scriptures foretold about Him?

options.text: That the Christ would suffer and rise, and that repentance and forgiveness would be preached to all nations in His name.
options.note: Luke 24:46-47 unites His suffering and rising with the worldwide mission.

options.text: That the Christ would suffer and rise from the dead on the third day.
options.note: True, but it omits the worldwide preaching of repentance and forgiveness that follows.

options.text: That the Messiah would reign over Israel in glory.
options.note: The stress here is on suffering, rising, and the mission to all nations, not national glory.

options.text: That the Scriptures had little to say about Him.
options.note: He says everything written about Him in all the Scriptures must be fulfilled.

quiz.q: What are the disciples commissioned and told to do?

options.text: To be witnesses and to wait in the city until they are clothed with power from on high.
options.note: Luke 24:48-49 gives both the witness role and the command to await the Spirit.

options.text: To be witnesses of Jesus' death and resurrection.
options.note: True, but it omits the command to wait for the promised power of the Spirit.

options.text: To go out immediately in their own strength.
options.note: They are told to wait for power from on high before going.

options.text: To keep the news within Jerusalem alone.
options.note: The mission is to all nations, beginning at Jerusalem, not confined to it.

options.text: Jesus lifts His hands, blesses them, and is carried up into heaven while blessing them.
options.note: Luke 24:50-51 shows the priestly blessing and the ascension.

options.text: Jesus is taken up into heaven before the disciples.
options.note: True, but it omits that He ascended in the very act of blessing them with lifted hands.

options.text: Jesus quietly disappears without a word.
options.note: He ascends openly while blessing them, not by silent vanishing.

options.text: Jesus departs, leaving the disciples in despair.
options.note: They return with great joy, not despair.

quiz.q: How do the disciples respond after Jesus ascends?

options.text: They worship Him and return to Jerusalem with great joy, praising God continually in the temple.
options.note: Luke 24:52-53 records worship, joy, and continual praise.

options.text: They return to Jerusalem filled with joy.
options.note: True, but it omits their worship of Jesus and continual praise in the temple.

options.text: They scatter to their homes in confusion.
options.note: The opposite: they gather in joyful worship, awaiting the Spirit.

options.text: They mourn the loss of their teacher.
options.note: They rejoice, for the ascension is His exaltation, not a loss.

questions: The risen Jesus greeted His failed disciples with, Peace be with you. How does it change you to know that Christ meets those who have failed Him not with reproach but with peace?
Jesus proved His resurrection was bodily, touchable, real. How does the physical reality of His rising strengthen your hope for your own resurrection and the renewal of all things?
He opened their minds to understand the Scriptures. Where do you need Christ to open your understanding of His word, rather than trying to grasp it by effort alone?
The disciples were told to wait for power from on high before going. Are you trying to serve God in your own strength, when He offers the power of His Spirit?

Jesus ascended while blessing His people, and they returned with great joy. Does the reign of the ascended Christ fill your daily life with that kind of worship and joy, and if not, what has crowded it out?

translationNotes.note: 'Touch Me and see, for a spirit does not have flesh and bones' renders a clause set off in the printed text with an em dash, here a comma. Jesus insists on the bodily reality of His resurrection against the thought that He is a ghost.
'The Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms' names the three divisions of the Hebrew Scriptures, a way of saying the whole Old Testament bears witness to Christ.

The 'promise of My Father' and 'power from on high' are the Holy Spirit, sent at Pentecost as recorded in Acts 2. Luke ends his Gospel with the ascension and picks up the same events at the start of Acts, binding the two volumes together.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 24:36-53. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, and speaker labels were skipped when assembling verse text. The em dash in verse 39 was converted to a comma. Sermon status set to none; no sermon from the trusted cluster was verified this session.
